reviews.review_text: one of the most satisfying combat systems/mechanics for any game I have played. another beautiful game and story produced by fromsoftware that well earned its game of the year title :)
Lives upto to its legendary status, had to get gud IRL. Combat never felt this satisfying.

Sekiro was the first Fromsoftware game I ever played, yeaaaa it was rough. Slowly but surely I built up skill in the game, but often times id get past the bosses through sheer willingness to throw myself at a brick wall until it came down. As with any souls like experience, each step of the way was agony followed by pure bliss and admiration of (well not at the time for me but certainly now after having played some more Fromsoft games) yet another Myazaki masterpiece. 

The fast paced yet so immersive and cohesive nature of the game, flushed out mechanics and sheer compatibility with mods was outstanding. The joy I felt after beating Isshin is a feeling i will never forget. I was also streaming for my friends which made the anticipation and the victory even more rewarding as there was a shared sense of admiration, excitement and happiness. 

I also highly recommend the Resurrection overhaul mod. Incredibly well done, makes the game harder but in an actual challenging way and not just artificial boosting of enemy stats. When I say this game is accommodating for mods, I mean genuine NEW moves and COMBOS by bosses that require real time adaptation. Incredibly fun and rewarding, should u git gud and win...

For a game that is easily 10/10 on a normal day, except for the day you have to "subdue" the burning bull.. The flames on its horns are foreshadowing for what you are about to experience, you shall burn in the deepest pit of hell. I hope the person that made the bull gets paid minimum wage. After playthrough #2 its not even a viable option to fight the bull anymore, this bull will single handedly make you regret the day you bought this game with the extra hp, posture and the damage, MY GOD THE DAMAGE combined with a hitbox that made me question my sanity. Around the end game theres a boss called demon of hatred, they should replace that boss with a bull and 2 extra lives, immediately a million times harder to beat, the game goes on and on about discipline on how you need to be patient and deflect all the enemy attacks, meanwhile the bull is like an actual brick wall and instead of the wall taking gradual damage, its just you getting proneboned where you either have to run around like an idiot and hit once every blue moon or just skip it if you dont wanna waste an hour of your life (unless its playthrough #1 without debuffs). 100 hours into the game, i could beat any boss without even attacking meanwhile this boss makes me gather more sweat in my ass crack than a 104 aussie summer.
definitely recommend the game if youre planning to skip the boss.
Its so amazing and so hard to play at the same time. Best game to test your gamer skills

Damn... what a ride this was. Truly one of the most rewarding  games ever. 7 Years later and for sure the best combat in a video game. Has this ruined gaming for me? will i ever find such beautiful combat again?

Incredible game with one of the best combat systems, a great story, and awesome bosses. It has less replayabilty than other Souls games since NG+ isn't very challenging, but getting all four endings is still worth it.

Don't give up when it gets hard. Learn the combat system, it's different from Dark Souls.

I hated this game for the longest time.... It's because I could not figure out why my new Dark Souls game from FromSoftware was so hard and designed bad compared to every other game.... then many moons later-WAHBBAMMMM!!! (inner monologue)_ ♥♥♥♥♥, it ain't a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ souls game, so don't be a ♥♥♥♥♥, get on your mouse and keyboard, and figure it out like the other ones.... and you better enjoy it, too!!_(end of inner monologue) ... then it got to be a much better experience when I realized that I had no choice but to GG at fkin parries or I just wasnt gonna beat the game I bought.
Then I began the adventure of a lifetime... It was amazing. Admittedly, I used a guide for the centipede thing cause I just could not get the timing, but everything was great... until....
I reached the Monkey/whatever the plural for Monkey is.....
I did not enjoy the gimmick of the chase or even the sneaky snake section prior. though they have grown on me over time, but when I get smashed by an Iron Golem's Fist Size worth of Feces......Bruh..... Ima /ill this ♥♥♥♥♥♥ primate, and ♥♥♥♥ the movie Planet of the Apes, ♥♥♥♥ is boring.
After reaching the mid endgame, there are very trying bosses and one month of depression, followed by a crossroads where you as a person have to ask yourself if you have a thing for cbt, some kind of fetish, or if it's just a phase-like when you painted your nails and died your hair in middle school, listening to marilyn manson, Nine Inch Nails, and slipknot everyday- and you wonder if life is worth living..... yeah that section is called Demon of Hatred.
After accepting that you, yourself, in fact, are the real Demon of Self-Hatred, and you finally somehow beat the flaming fukboy..... you can then endure an objectively harder boss, the Sword Saint.
Sword Saint imo isnt as hard as DoH, but then again, I think the final Boss Ishin, Sword Saint is ♥♥♥♥♥♥ awesome no matter how many times he's spread my cheeks, cause I'd spread em for that boss anytime....
Wait... Nah, I would. 10/10 Must Play, Dont be a person so ignorant that you'd deny yourself the satisfaction and honor of this experience, over what, no difficulty mode? It isnt like Dark Souls? Your Mom Just Passed Yesterday? Pssh, You make me sick! Just Play the ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ Game Bruh, Dont be A Puss, I wish I could play Sekiro instead of attend my mother's funeral.... You have the world in your hands and you dont even know it...

Hope this Helps, GG Scrub

Such a rewarding game to master. Combat is the best I've experienced. The better you get, the more satisfying the fights are. So addicting that I finished 2 NG+ runs in a day after I finished my first playthrough. Story is great, your decisions can change the ending. Gameplay heavily emphasizes the aspects of being a Shinobi which is awesome.

Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice is one of the most intense, rewarding, and unforgettable gaming experiences I have ever had. From the very beginning, the game throws the player into a brutal and unforgiving world, but instead of feeling unfair, every challenge feels meaningful. Every defeat teaches you something, and every victory feels truly earned. That is what makes this game so special.

What impressed me the most was the combat system. It is easily one of the best combat systems ever created in a video game. Instead of relying on simple attacks or dodging all the time, the game focuses heavily on timing, precision, and patience. The posture system completely changes the way fights work and makes every duel feel cinematic and personal. When swords clash and sparks fly across the screen, the battles feel like scenes straight out of a samurai movie. Winning against a difficult boss after dozens of attempts creates a feeling of satisfaction that very few games can achieve.

The boss fights are absolutely incredible. Almost every major enemy feels unique and memorable, both in design and gameplay. Some bosses are terrifying, some are elegant, and others are simply overwhelming, but all of them force the player to improve. Instead of becoming stronger through grinding levels, you become stronger by mastering the mechanics yourself. That makes progress feel much more rewarding and personal.

The atmosphere of the game is another reason why I loved it so much. The world is dark, mysterious, and beautiful at the same time. Ancient temples, snowy mountains, burning castles, and hidden forests all create an unforgettable setting inspired by Japanese history and mythology. The soundtrack perfectly supports the atmosphere without being overwhelming, and the sound design during combat makes every sword strike feel powerful.

I also really appreciated the storytelling. The game does not explain everything directly to the player, which makes the world feel deeper and more realistic. The story of Wolf, the main character, is emotional and tragic, centered around loyalty, honor, and sacrifice. Even though he speaks very little, his journey becomes surprisingly emotional over time. The relationship between Wolf and Kuro gives the story real heart and makes the player care about the outcome.

Another thing that makes the game stand out is how satisfying movement feels. Using the grappling hook to move across rooftops and explore hidden areas adds a sense of freedom and speed that perfectly fits the ninja fantasy. Exploration always feels rewarding because there are secrets hidden everywhere, from optional bosses to valuable upgrades and lore details.

What truly amazed me is how the game respects the player. It never holds your hand or lowers its difficulty to make things easier. Instead, it challenges you to adapt, learn, and stay determined. At first, the difficulty can feel overwhelming, but over time you realize the game is teaching you discipline and focus. Once the combat finally “clicks,” the experience becomes almost addictive.

Overall, Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice is a masterpiece. It combines brutal but fair gameplay, breathtaking world design, unforgettable bosses, and an emotionally powerful story into one incredible experience. It is not just a game about fighting enemies — it is a game about perseverance, improvement, and mastering yourself. Few games have ever made me feel so challenged, frustrated, excited, and proud at the same time. Even after finishing it, the experience stays in your mind for a long time.

This review is specifically for ADHD folks.
Great gameplay, I would recommend giving it a shot if you care about playing GOTYs especially on sale.

If you suffer from severe ADHD, anhedonia and short-term memory problems will make this game more of a chore than anything. 
I found the combat relatively easy as I have great reflexes due to my medication. 
One of the main reasons I got the game was combat, and how satisfying people made out learning and beating bosses to be.
I have beaten the game twice, and personally haven't felt this satisfaction after learning bosses and besting them, just mild annoyance and relief that the fight is over.
This is an ADHD problem and not a Sekiro problem, but it should be a significant consideration if you have symptoms similar to mine and want to try this game out.
I would recommend reading about bosses online instead of trying to learn them yourself as you will just be batting heads against your memory issues. It's not a pleasant experience.

I'm not entirely sure if I recommend the game, but I don't regret playing it.

The gameplay is good, especially after a few accessibility tweaks on PC like disabling camera reset and FOV changes. Otherwise the default FOV gives me nausea and headaches.

I can't say much about the characters, story and setting as they don't appeal to me and were not the reason I decided to play the game. Except for O'Rin she's a highlight of the game.

Overall somewhat enjoyable, maybe. Beware if you suffer from severe ADHD and are medicated like me.

The Demon of Hatred spiritually deserves to be cheesed. Never believe otherwise.
Hesitation is defeat.

Possibly the easiest, most difficult game I’ve ever played.
Enemies show absolutely no mercy when any mistake or lapse in judgment is made, which almost always results in an immediate death.
However the parrying and counter mechanics make up for that by adding possibly the most forgiving system I’ve seen in a game to date. 
Although the game’s not for the faint of heart, and incredibly unforgiving at times, it easily makes up for it by being one of the best, most intuitive gaming experiences money can buy.

Took me a long time to beat this. I really enjoyed going through the whole process. From looking at other people's reviews it looks like they finished it in the about 30 hours so I was thinking that's how long the game is going to be but it took me what 120 hours. But I'm glad it took that long. I did take a lot longer with the bosses. I really wanted to learn how to beat them and not just have some kind of cheat way of beating them.
I would recommend this game and I would recommend taking your time at it.

Absolute masterpiece, totally different from the usual FromSoftware souls formula. Learn and adapt to the new playstyle and you will have a lot of fun. It took me 30hrs for the first playthrough and it was well worth the time and money spent. Not a single regret, a must play if you enjoy difficult games. Approach the bosses with a more of a rhythm-puzzle solving mentality and you will be good.

I'm speechless with this game... What a ride it was, i loved it throughout the whole journey.. This feels really different to other fromsoft games because you don't grow stats or anything like that, but the feeling of learning the fight, parrying your way into submission, is unmatched! i love the utter aggression that the game incentivizes you to go for.. And i loved the setting, feudal Japan with magic and lore im a sucker for these.. And this game is gorgeous, wonderfully voiced in japanese, with crazy art design.. I see how it won goty back in 2019.. Im really happy i got to experience this masterpiece! Thank you fromsoftware!

Not at all as hard as people make it out to be. Defeated most bosses first try, aside from Inner Isshin & True Monk. Combat flows beautifully, abilities are all unqiue and useful. Story is a bit weak compared to other games but is very good for a FromSoft title. Solid 7/10

It's a different experience. Core combat is deflecting enemy sword and breaking up enemy posture. But also understand that deflecting is not everything. Some enemies have high posture, so you need to look for opening also. If you are a normie like me, expect to die few times to figure out a boss combat. Combat is challenging but it's fair. Game gives you multiple options to make combat easier. From my overall experience, I recommend.

This is, without a doubt, the best game Fromsoftware has ever put out. The combat takes time to adjust too especially if you played other souls games. I bounced off this three times before I stubbornly forced myself to play. It finally clicked when I got to Lady Butterfly, and I didn't put it down. Don't be afraid to look up guides, and be willing to get crushed until the patterns finally click and you deflect every strike levied your way.

Combat: 10/10
Bosses: 8/10
Progression: 9/10
World/Exploration: 8/10
Story: 7/10
Overall: A+

Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice delivered one of the most rewarding gameplay experiences I've ever had. The combat system is the best I've experienced in gaming—every deflect, Mikiri Counter, and boss victory feels earned. The progression from struggling against Genichiro to eventually defeating the final boss was incredibly satisfying. While I don't think its world, story, exploration, or boss roster quite reach the heights of Elden Ring, Zelda, or The Last of Us, Sekiro's focused design and unmatched combat make it an easy recommendation. I finished the game in about 45 hours, thoroughly exploring each area along the way, and even though it doesn't crack my personal S-tier, it's an outstanding A+ experience that every action game fan should play.

Despite Elden Ring being the best FromSoftware game ever created and, in my opinion, surpassing Sekiro in nearly every category, Sekiro slightly edges it out in one crucial area: combat. Elden Ring gave me my favorite bosses, world, exploration, and sense of adventure, but Sekiro's swordplay is simply unmatched. No other game has made me feel the same level of growth and mastery from beginning to end.  If you enjoy challenging action games and want to experience arguably the greatest melee combat system ever made, this is an easy recommendation.

Sick game, all around 

great atmosphere, not gonna take you 300+ hours, fast combat, when you get it down it makes you feel like a RL ninja, cant reccommend enough, just be warned this sh*ts HARD

So, Guys Fiinally I have completed this game by playing this my ass breaking Game Sekiro.If you think you are gonna win,lose your hopes alrady because this game requires most of your parry,deflect,dodge skills of your response. 
Then you have to grind in the whole game for the divine confetti.


at the end, every bosses you fight is a something relaxing thing.

I hate just 1 thing in the game,if you have complete 1 ending ,you cannot revert back to other endings at the same time.You have to do from the start.THIS IS THE MOST HATRED IN THE WORLD.
⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ 5/5

Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice is a masterpiece.

Combat: ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
The best sword combat system I have ever experienced in a video game. Every deflection, counter, and deathblow feels incredibly satisfying.

Boss Fights: ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
Some of the greatest bosses in gaming history. Each fight is a test of skill, patience, and mastery of the combat system.

Story: ⭐⭐⭐⭐☆
A compelling tale inspired by Japanese history and mythology, with memorable characters and multiple endings.

World Design: ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
Beautiful environments, excellent level design, and rewarding exploration enhanced by the grappling hook.

Graphics & Art Direction: ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
The visuals are stunning, but the art direction is what truly makes the world unforgettable.

Sound & Music: ⭐⭐⭐⭐☆
Atmospheric and immersive, perfectly complementing every battle and location.

Difficulty: ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
Challenging but fair. The game rewards learning and improvement rather than grinding.

Replayability: ⭐⭐⭐⭐☆
Multiple endings, New Game+, and achievement hunting provide plenty of reasons to return.

Overall: ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐

Sekiro doesn't just test your reflexes—it teaches you discipline, patience, and perseverance. Few games provide a sense of accomplishment as rewarding as finally defeating a boss that once seemed impossible.

"Hesitation is defeat."

Highly recommended.
by far the greatest game fromsoftware has released, and also the greatest soulslike game while we are at it.
Mainly got is cause a friend wouldn't stop talking about it, he plays pretty much all souls/soul type games. Can confirm for me that this game is indeed difficult for me, but I did have fun playing
Probably FromSoft's best game.  I like the Dark Souls games, but this one is special.
There are some genuinely cool ideas and boss fights, but I don't think the overall package deserves the pedestal people put it on.
its the best fromsoft game, it isnt really a debate ngl

I'm a huge fan of the FromSoft games, with my first two being Dark Souls 3 and Dark Souls Remastered. Sekiro is just something different entirely. The combat is addicting, the combat arts and prosthetic tools allow for fun, creative approaches to dealing with mobs and bosses. The story, while not being very understandable on the surface in classic FromSoft fashion, is beautiful and detailed. 

It's FromSoft's best game imo, and who doesn't like the premise of Sengoku-era Japan rolled into a FromSoftware package? I've 100% this game two or three times and I find myself coming back to it every so often. It's just. that. good.

Greatest Game Ever Made, Deserves a Sequel. No combat in any other single game is on the level that this one is. Hands Down. Not an Opinion. A Fact. You Think Otherwise?

Guess You Never Got Good...
A journey towards mastery. More of an exercise in meditation than a video game. Brilliant and one of a kind.

I just finished Sekiro and I must say this is by far the most fun I've ever had losing to a boss, the combat is so fluid and so perfect that any other game with parry systems pale in comparison. The bosses in this game were so much harder than I was expecting but it was so rewarding beating each one. The story, though short, was beautiful and I really found myself caring for the characters I got to chatting with. This is by far one of the greatest games ever and is the peak of combat systems, 10/10. Do Not Hesitate.

i gotta say the obvious but:
It's soulslike
It's samurai
you either hate these things or this game is for you

[h1]A masterpiece—even the nitpicks are minor.[/h1]
[h2]10/10[/h2]

[b]Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice[/b] is, hands down, one of the most fun and rewarding games I have ever played. The core of the experience is a combat system that is simply flawless, turning every single boss fight into a lethal dance of reflexes and precision. A massive triumph of the game is how it handles failure: dying never feels frustrating. Enemy movesets are so incredibly readable that when you fall, it’s always obvious that it was your own mistake rather than some unfairness in the enemy's moveset.

[hr][/hr]

[b]PROS:[/b]
[list]
[*] [b]Impeccable controls:[/b] Extremely responsive and precise input registration.
[*] [b]Masterful combat:[/b] A clean, straightforward system of parries and deflections that never feels shallow or repetitive.
[*] [b]Legendary boss fights:[/b] Epic, memorable encounters across both main bosses and optional sub-bosses.
[*] [b]Incredible attention to detail:[/b] Great care put into mini-bosses and the attack patterns of regular enemies alike.
[*] [b]Striking art direction:[/b] Top-tier visuals, atmospheric environments, and special effects.
[/list]

[b]CONS (really splitting hairs to find flaws):[/b]
[list]
[*] [b]Steep learning curve:[/b] The beginning can be brutal and requires a healthy dose of patience to overcome.
[*] [b]Great replayability, but not infinite:[/b] The game's biggest strength —the single, unreplaceable weapon that allows for such a polished combat system— eventually becomes a bit restrictive in New Game+ runs. Since you can't radically change your build or playstyle, it can start to feel rigid after 4 or 5 consecutive runs (personally, I've done 8 or 9 runs and always had a blast, but the structural limitation is there).
[*][spoiler][b]An exception in the boss roster:[/b] There is an optional post-game boss whose mechanics seem to clash with Sekiro's core parry system, making it feel artificially difficult and frustrating for many players.[/spoiler]
[*] [b]Linear narrative:[/b] The story is relatively simple and doesn't bring anything particularly groundbreaking to the gaming landscape.
[/list]

[hr][/hr]

[b]Final Thoughts & Evaluation[/b]
An action masterpiece that comes dangerously close to gameplay perfection. If you are looking for an honest yet punishing challenge backed by a flawless combat loop, this is an absolute must-buy.

100% completed. Every achievement. Every ending. Every boss. Every prayer bead. Every memory.

Sekiro is one of the few games where getting all the achievements never felt like a chore. The combat starts out brutal, but once everything clicks, every fight becomes a fast-paced dance of steel. Instead of dodging around enemies waiting for an opening, you're forced to stand your ground, learn their patterns, and break their posture through pure skill.

The boss roster is incredible. Fights that seemed impossible at first became some of the most satisfying victories I've ever had in gaming. Nothing beats the feeling of finally defeating a boss that had been destroying you for hours.

The progression is also fantastic. Looking back at enemies that terrified me in the early game and effortlessly defeating them later really showed how much I had improved as a player, not just how much stronger my character had become.

Even after multiple playthroughs for all the endings, the game never got old. Each run felt faster, smoother, and more rewarding than the last. By the end, I wasn't just playing Sekiro—I felt like I had actually mastered it.

If you've ever quit because a boss felt impossible, keep going. One day you'll beat that boss, start a new playthrough, and wonder how you ever struggled against them in the first place.

Hesitation is defeat.

When this game was announced back in 2018(?) I was disappointed. I wanted a Soulslike. I didn't even try until around 2023 and it didn't stick. This year I decided to start working through my backlog of games and when it came time for Sekiro I started a new game and locked in. This may be my favorite game of all time.
This was my first soulslike game and it was honestly a master piece. Tough as nails but also fair, very strong recommendation for those who aren't afraid of a challenge.
Oh boy... what a game that was! Easily a 10/10 game. It surely belongs to one of my favorites from now on. FromSoftware outdone themselves once again. I fully recommend this masterpiece of a game!

In my opinion, the absolute best Souls-like game, ever!
I love Elden Ring, but the combat, progression, setting, and story in Sekiro are for me much more compelling. I wish they made something like a sequel with an open world, like ER.
A fun frantic action game from one of the best in the biz.  It's a souls like but the combat and story are very different from the typical souls game.

Uma segunda chance para um dos jogos em que eu mais apanhei no tutoras me fez perceber o quanto esse aqui é, de longe o melhor soulslike da from e agora um dos meus jogos favoritos
Você apanha tanto em alguns bosses até decorar cada movimento e cada erro seu, e uma hora isso deixa de ser ódio e vira orgulho quando finalmente consegue derrotá-los.

15/10

maybe best action game for me
Might have some boring area like minamoto shrine but overall is very good
Combat is perfect by far, sadly there is no DLC

Great game, albeit with some problems. 10/10 experiences, but if your point of entry was Elden Ring, you could see that this is less streamlined (I have beaten all the bosses so I'm not salty neither it's a "git gud" issue). There are certain problems with camera in small arenas (improved upon in Elden Ring),  hooks could be working better, some assets are re-used etc. Contrary to what many people say, it IS a souls games (sites of grice, experience lost on death, healing, buffs, you CAN level although you're stuck in 1 class: you can improve attack power, poise dmg, hp, etc; you just can't choose a different class). The fighting system is not that different. Parry windows are extremely generous (0.3 second) IF YOU DON'T spam the parry button. The game suffers from the same problems as Elden Ring: extremely long input buffers/queues, so you're punished for button mashing and panic pressing. IF YOU DON'T PRESS BUTTONS that you don't need to press, the game is easy. You are also stuck in long recovery frames (and moderate starting frames), so it's very similar to Elden Ring. Of course it is the other way around and Sekiro inspired boss desing in ER: you have to be looking for openings and the bosses are very agressive. 

All in all:
Experience 10/10, Great Care and love 10/10, but also rage meter 9/10 (in Elden Ring my rage meter was 4/10). It's not necesserily harder than ER, but more enraging. Also the games doesn't have a proper tutorial and you have to wait till Genichiro till it finally clicks (10-15 hours) because that is the first boss that has long combos that you have to parry. It's hard to learn that before when fighting other mini-bosses. ALSO: there is a lot of stealth, like in Hitman or Splinter Cell.

Do I recommend it? Yes, it's a great game, but you have to be VEEEERY patient with it.
It's like the final exam before graduation, you can't name yourself as a gamer before beat it.

Fantastic game. I don't have time to play games as much anymore so i tend to complete a game and move on, but this one keeps me coming back. Replaying bosses and restarting the campaign over and over. The only flaw is not having a sequel already.
This game is ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ amazing, first game I have ever gotten 100% in. It was so fun to play, I can't wait to play it again in a few years.
No combat system comes close to matching the level of satisfaction in Sekiro, pure ejaculation hitting a string of deflections.

[h2]Pretty good game overall with some notable shortcomings[/h2]
Fromsoft did an awesome job with this one. I quite enjoyed my time with Sekiro. The game was sufficiently challenging and the combat system was extremely well crafted. It feels really smooth to the point it almost becomes a rhythmic game. The hitboxes are clean on almost all the bosses so everything has a really great flow to it. Some boss designs notably those of [spoiler]Genichiro, Owl, True Monk, O' Rin of the Water and Isshin[/spoiler] are insanely well done so much so that you might find yourself just admiring how beautiful their swordsmanship is. What is definitely quite well reflected is how their fighting style also matches with their respective lore implications, which brings me to the story itself.

The story has been told in a rather straightforward fashion if you consider fromsoft's usual quirks and it fits the game pretty well. A pretty good job has been done here although I feel that the English VA leaves things quite lacking. I wish I didn't find Japanese VA along with English subtitles an odd combination otherwise that option would have been a much better way to experience the story of the game. I just don't prefer a language that I don't understand. Perhaps all these factors might have attributed to me not getting quite invested in the lore itself. So, I was mostly focussed on the combat aspect of the game while the story kind of took a back seat in that regards. Still, I don't really find any major flaw in the story itself but rather its delivery due to the reasons I mentioned before.

The locations though, I feel they are nothing to write home about if I am being honest. Yes, they are pretty scenic in their own ways and others might feel differently but for me, most of it left kind of a bland taste. [spoiler]Ashina Outskirts and Sunken Valley[/spoiler] are notable exceptions though. I think those are the only two places I really enjoyed exploring and loved the way they were crafted.

Now, coming over to the combat which forms the majority of the game, and this is where most of my grievances lie. Whilst its been quite well executed with some great bosses as I mentioned before, it has some really annoying quirks that account for major setbacks. The core of the game revolves around the deflection mechanic and resurrection ability, but the very first boss you face violates those principles as the [spoiler]Chained Ogre[/spoiler] very much feels like a Souls boss. Not to mention, he can sometimes throw you off the cliff thanks to a bad boss arena as well rendering the resurrection ability useless in some cases. All in all, great first boss for a game all about deflection. 

I find similar violations in other bosses of the game like [spoiler]Guardian Ape, Headless Ape and Demon of Hatred.[/spoiler] They needlessly ruin the challenge the game presents and are simply hard just for the sake of it. Defeating them does not bring any sense of accomplishment because you are just glad the annoyance is finally over and you can move on. Even when it comes to the bosses I initially complimented, I did not like the fact that a lot of times their 2nd/3rd phases would simply be minor extensions of the 1st phase or at times be completely similar with just an addition of the boss now being able to cast a status effect. It feels like needless padding to draw out the fight in order to make the boss seem more difficult than it really is. I would have loved some major additions to a 2nd phase fight with new movesets as that would have provided a great challenge and made the game much more fun. [spoiler]The 3rd phase of both Genichiro and Isshin provide some welcome additions, but in this case, they don't make the boss any stronger. It instead provides you with a huge advantage in the Lightning Reversal Technique that allows you to defeat them easily in this phase. I would have loved to see it instead become more difficult or atleast the technique itself being more difficult to cast.[/spoiler]

Coming back to the location aspect, [spoiler]I also find the use of Ashina Castle quite literally at 3 different points of the story quite jarring. It honestly comes off as a lazy decision because there are not any significant major changes to speak of. Well, lore implications would obviously suggest that there is but the game does not capture it all that well. So, the execution very much comes off as a bit lazy.[/spoiler] 

Overall, the game was quite fun and I had a good time. These were some nitpicks that I picked up on my end which imo make it fall short of becoming a masterpiece. Otherwise, it is a well made game that I recommend anyone looking for a challenge to give a try.
From Software at its best. Elden Ring is great, but this one takes the cake.

I hate this game.
I am a huge fan of From Software's games, beat Dark Souls 1 2 and 3, Elden Ring and Armoured Core VI. And I am quite bad at the games.
The things I like about them isn't the difficulty, but the exploration and atmosphere. I don't win my battles with skill, I win them with endurance. Not with reaction speed, but with battle tactics.
Every other From Software game allows you to explore the game further, make adjustments to your build, or even play in co-op for a bit to get some levels and experience. Sekiro doesn't.
Either you are able to handle the fight, or you don't. If you have difficulties with some boss(es) your only option is to bang your head against that wall.
I had bosses which I could not beat for literal months. I came back to it every few days or so, tried, failed, farmed a bit to get another skill, tried again, failed again and gave up. Everything that normally lets me succeed (like exploring every bit of the game I can to this point, trying to just survive at first to learn the patterns, or trying again after some sleep) just wasn't enough.
It's funny that many players dislike the longer runbacks of From Software's older games, but seem to very much like multi-phase-bossfights, because for me every bit of progress was behind an agonizingly long first or maybe even second phase that I had already beat many times and just dragged the process of learning into unnecessary lengths. I had many sessions that just made me feel like total ♥♥♥♥, because I literally made zero progress for days. So for me I would say that the game would have been much much more enjoyable without many of the last boss phases, especially as there's a common trend in Sekiro for the last boss phases to just add some ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ magic/lightning/fire stuff that's not as visually clear as the rest of the fight.
Besides the bosses, I enjoyed every bit of it. The exploration is pretty good, the game is just beautiful, even has a relatively understandable story and the combat system itself is obviously a masterpiece.
So I definitely see why they pushed the difficulty that much, but for me personally it was just too much. And I think I can say that, since I actually beat the game by now no matter what.
What's interesting (as is with lot's of their games) is that on a second playthrough, I just started recently I first-tried a majority of the bosses. As impossible they were for me to learn, they equally impossible to un-learn, it seems. And since on my second playthrough the agonizing parts aren't there, I can enjoy it now much much more.
I love this game.
Great game, combat is super addicting, only problem is that damned ape, emma.
Great game. Love the melee skill ceiling combat. Hoping for a new FromSoftware game to come out. The switch 2 exclusive was a little disappointing. Not a console gamer.
not only the best from soft game ive ever played, perhaps the best game of all time. and i say that with 2500 hours in fallout new vegas, ark survival evolved and many many more favorites.
very good, but with a steep early difficulty curve. it also plays nothing like other fromsoftware games, so don't expect skill to translate from one game to another.

Sekiro is easily the best game I've ever played. There's just something about the feel of the combat that I can't get from any other game. This game reminded me that I am capable of overcoming any obstacle, and that staying in the fight is the best way to get results. Defeating Lady Butterfly, Genichiro Ashina, and Isshin Ashina were peak gaming for me and I'll never forget that final moment of the game. I don't know who I need to thank for this experience, but thanks.

My favorite action game ever. The story is actually coherent in a fromsoft game and the setting is gorgeous. The highlight of the game is the combat system. The more you improve the more opportunities you find to be even more aggressive. There isn't a single game like it out there that allows you to be on the offense so frequently. And when you do need to defend yourself, the parry isn't your only tool available. As you become more familiar with the enemy movesets you can find times where dodging and jumping are actually the better moves against certain attacks. It's not just a parry simulator as a lot of people who misunderstand the combat system think.

I also love being able to animation cancel, I wish more games followed suit. It allows me to push myself to find more opportunities while not having to waste my time by getting "punished" for testing the limits of my character against different foes. I really hope even if there isn't a sequel that there is some sort of spiritual successor eventually. Give me more sekiro-likes and fewer souls-likes please.
no joke the best game i ever played maybe only elden ring can keep up with it pretty hard at first but you will learn to flow of the game pretty quickly and has some of the best bosses ever

sekiro has a combat system that actually forces you to learn to play the game on its terms, and the satisfaction from learning to do that instead of panic slamming buttons or bruteforcing your way through combat is immense. the learning curve is admittedly steep, but once you slow down and calmly focus, the rhythmic cling clang sound of attacks and deflections becomes so meditative you almost forget what nightmare of a day wolf is having.

YOU LOVE SAMURAI?
YOU WANNA FEEL LIKE THE BOSS?
YOU DREAM OF DISHING OUT ANIME STYLE SPECIAL MOVES?
GO PLAY SEKIRO NOW ON YOUR PC, PS4 OR XBOX. 
Jokes aside, Sekiro is amazing. parry timings are smooth and you feel every single hit. Just beware, casually, Sekrio is the hardest fromsoft game. it will take time until you go into a flow and really start getting and learning the way to play. and honestly, sekiro is no souls like. its a rythm game. once youve mastered and memorized the bosses, it gets a lot, and i mean A LOT easier. Sekiro doesnt have a lot of build variety, as you only have that one katana, although you can still have a bit with the skill tree and prosthesis. i think the replayability is only like up to 4 times, for the 4 endings as you will have most likely mastered the combat by then and it wont be much of a challenge anymore.

This was a very fun game for me to try, considering I was never really any good at Parrying in other games. But, this game has such great boss designs that when playing against normal enemies and the bosses especially, you get a feeling of their rhythm and how they move around that it really just turns into a rhythm game funny enough. Furthermore, it also keeps in mind the mechanics that the players have at their disposal against the bosses to such an extent to where you can shut down bosses moves completely with certain strategies, such as lady butterfly's illusions in her second faze with the snap seeds. Moreover, this goes to show how well crafted the bosses are in this game and dare I say most if not all of fromsoftware's games, and how much fun they are to fight.
Loved the combat! Once it clicks, it's the most satisfying swordplay experience in a video game.
Refreshing take on souls-like. It's also great to have Japanese storytelling for once, where themes and stories are implicit and full of nuance.

Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice is an action-adventure game developed by FromSoftware and published by Activision. It stands apart from the studio’s other titles by focusing on precision-based sword combat, mobility, and strict mastery of timing rather than character builds or RPG stat progression.

The combat system is the defining feature. Instead of traditional health attrition, fights are built around posture management, deflections, and perfectly timed counters. Every encounter becomes a tense rhythm duel where hesitation is punished and precision is rewarded. Once mastered, the system delivers some of the most satisfying melee combat in modern gaming, especially during boss fights that demand adaptation and discipline.

Exploration is tightly designed, with vertical traversal, grappling hook movement, and interconnected areas that encourage experimentation. The world design is more focused and less open than other FromSoftware titles, but this contributes to its pacing and clarity. The narrative is delivered through environmental storytelling and sparse dialogue, reinforcing its mythic interpretation of Sengoku-era Japan.

Visually, the game holds up well due to strong art direction and animation quality. On modern hardware, Sekiro runs smoothly and can maintain a stable 60 FPS experience at high settings, ensuring consistent responsiveness that is crucial for its timing-based combat system.

The difficulty curve is steep, and the game is uncompromising toward player mistakes, which may discourage some players. However, its design philosophy is deliberate and tightly executed, rewarding persistence and learning.

Score: 9.5/10 – A masterclass in precision combat design, offering unmatched swordplay, intense boss encounters, and one of the most refined action systems in gaming.

Clearly one of the best games ever made by FromSoftware, Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice takes us into the late Sengoku era through a fantastical feudal Japan setting. Gameplay-wise, honestly, Sekiro’s combat is far better than both Dark Souls and Elden Ring, because the game truly forces players to fight with rhythm. Defense feels far more satisfying thanks to the emphasis on deflecting attacks instead of simply dodging, making combat revolve around tempo, timing, and reading your opponent’s flow, since reckless aggression can quickly be punished by the AI. 

Even though it has some flaws — especially in the story, which feels like it could’ve been expanded further — Sekiro still delivers an engaging and compelling tale about the loyalty of a shinobi in a cold world where almost everyone carries moral ambiguity. One antagonist, Genichiro Ashina, especially managed to earn some sympathy from me, since his love for his homeland (or perhaps more accurately, his domain/clan in this context) drives him to do absolutely anything for its survival. The story also feels deeply rooted in Japanese themes, particularly the contrast between immortality and impermanence, supported by symbols and mythological elements that fit perfectly with Japanese folklore — which makes sense, considering the game was made by a Japanese studio after all. 

The combat can also feel almost unforgiving at times, as bosses often need only a few hits to kill you, making many fights feel like they demand near-perfect execution. Still, while that can definitely be seen as a criticism, it’s also impossible to deny that this very demand for perfection is what makes defeating a boss feel so incredibly satisfying afterward (except Demon of Hatred — ♥♥♥♥ that guy). Overall, the journey through Ashina is genuinely unforgettable. Whether it’s bullying Genichiro, raging against the Headless, or losing sanity after dropping 10,000 sen, the game constantly creates memorable moments. 

A solid 10/10, and an absolutely deserved Game of the Year winner!

High mobility, tight combat, great characters and world, fantastic story.

Sekiro is a polished standalone story, using a different combat system from Dark Souls. This aspect will trip a lot of new players, but the moment it clicks changes everything. Every fight after becomes a satisfying dance of aggression and parrying.

The boss fights are by far the biggest highlight, featuring some of the best fights in all of Fromsoft's games. Even comparing the ones in Elden Ring.

It has cemented its place as one of my favourite games.
poop game, i got spanked by the shotgun enemy and the water still moves while the games paused. genuinely unplayable. Still better than nightreign

Here in 2026, finishing what I started in 2020, I can only say the fighting system is incredibly satisfying. The boss fights are epic, and I learned that Gachiin's Sugar, Gachiin's Spiritfall, and the Puppeteer Ninjutsu are your best tools. This is a game where you are a Shinobi (stealth), not a Samurai. When enemies spot you and there are many of them, you kill one or two, apply Puppeteer and fight or escape—otherwise, you’re dead.

This year I felt the game was even better than during my first playthrough, and I finally reached 100% achievements. I can only hope that From Software creates a continuation or a similar game.

This is only my second soul-like game and I have heard this one was very different. The first I played was Lies of P and I loved the parry mechanic. Was I satisfied? More than I could have ever imagined! This game is incredibly hard during the first playthrough because there is very little customization and very well planned skill upgrades throughout the game. As a result: it is YOU the player who needs to improve. I must have died between 30 and 100 times per boss (the final boss took me 2h30 to beat). Today, I just platinum the game and beat that last boss in 2 tries. The feeling of being in control of the fight is quite exhilarating. I highly recommend this game to whoever (1) likes challenging games, (2) wants to play a highly satisfying (although initially frustrating) battle mechanic game, and (3) likes a very immersive japanese setting fictional story.
This game goes for sure among my top games of all time.

First Souls game I ever played definitely one of the best video games ever created. I wish there would be a Sekiro 2 or something. I love the paring mechanism and wished it was also adapted into other dark souls games.

As someone who had never played a FromSoft game before I tried out Elden Ring and it just wasn't for me. Not wanting to give up on such an acclaimed studio based on one game I tried Sekiro next on a whim and was blown away. This has some of the best katana combat I have ever experienced and the game consistently rewards you for thinking like a shinobi to accomplish goals rather than brute forcing things. I love this game and if you aren't afraid of a challenge I definitely say it is worth playing.

I usually play a game once and move on after beating it, but this game is so good and so fun, that I decided to have 3 more playthroughs in a row.

This is personally my favorite Fromsoft game, I've never had a game where the combat is this satisfying and euphoric.

A classic, fun combat, stealth is kind of bad, fun enemies although lacking a bit in diversity and amount. Soundtrack and environments are ok but not as good as other FromSoft games. Some issues (HDR, couple crashes). Would definitely recommend overall.

Not only is this the only game I've replayed more than once it's the only Steam game I wanted to Platinum. Honestly, 10/10 Story, solid character development too, but neither of those would encourage me to leave a review. I'm leaving a review and calling this the single best solo-player game I've encountered for one simple reason.... The Replay-ability. A lot of people, probably even most players will tell you this one has no Replayability because you've learned everything's attack patterns to even survive much less progress, but as someone who never replays any story games and somebody those replayed Sekiro 8 times, I'm telling you this is Gold. Once you learn it, you go from taking 20+ hours to reach the Shura ending to doing it in 2. Every single voice line from a dying boss feels 10000X more personal and what has become easy for you still feels like overcoming what you know is nearly impossible. Once you master it to the point you don't need a margin for error (because there never is any) you feel John Wick, you're not really the BoogeyMan you are what the Devine Heir Lord Kuro sends to KILL the BoogeyMan! I beat the game in under 25 hours, Platinumed it in under 60 then played more than another 120 and I'm still likely coming back to it a few time a year.
Good game however, it is frustrating at the start getting used to the game but later on it gets really satisfying parrying bosses. Would recommend.

The game is great, the combat is intense, especially the deflect, which is extremely difficult, but you can also gain that if you can parry/deflect quickly. The game also shows you have your own and your opponent's fascage bar. Unlike in Elden Ring or Dark Souls where you have to deplete the boss's health to finish them off, in Sekiro Shadows Die Twice, the faster you are, the stronger you are. I should have finished this game before 10 hours, but because I was so focused on looting and farming, it took me over 110 hours =))). The game is great, nothing to lose. I'm preparing to play the new game plus mode to get more loot and farm items to upgrade the prosthetic arm and all of Wolf's skills and other endings. That's an achievement, right?

This games is one of the best out there you can play. I had a lot of frustration at first with this game but that’s simply because I wasn’t focused on learning the fights and getting better with my reactions, I was just mashing the deflect button and hoping to win.

As opposed to a game like Elden Ring, which is exploration focused, putting combat as a second priority, Sekiro's main selling point is the combat, and it DEFINITELY delivers on that aspect. The combat is fluid, addictive, fast, and for once I find myself having a lot of fun even on bosses I would otherwise be raging against on any other game. Combat aside, the world building and level design is also fantastic, but I would buy this game for the combat mechanics alone.

10/10

Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice is easily the summit of where soulslike games have been leading since demon souls. It manages to surpass the pitfalls of the traditional soulsbourne games and Elden Ring by allowing defense to become a form of offense. Enemy and boss combos all feel very natural with very few exceptions and countering bosses becomes more of a matter of learning how to dance with what they decide to throw out in their move sets and mix-ups rather than following a scripted few moves while waiting for the right attack window. The simple move set plays greatly into the game's strengths by prioritizing mastery of the game's mechanics over finding cheap strategies to win fights. This is a very difficult game but that great difficulty is met with even greater satisfaction in mastering the game.
Pretty good game when you like being ♥♥♥♥♥♥ in the ass by a new boss every hour or so

I would say that this game is one the hardest souls games i have ever played from the way it start you have to adapt the mechanics of the game YOU NEED TO bc you will die many times and you just suck, this game teach me to have more patience and not rush yourself , I love it so much and the history adds more into the game as well to know more about the lonely wolf , nameless sekiro , GOAT GAME
Hard game that gets way easier as you learn to play. Great story and great gameplay. You just have to experience it.

Just killed Isshin for the third time for platinum. People ask you to play passively in this game. The thing is you have to play as aggressive as possible. Don't give your opponent any freedom. Also Listen to sounds, the moment your sword's hits (ting) turn (tang), deflect then plan your next move according to boss's attacks. Mikiri Counter is your bestfriend, master it. Don't rush, this game will make you feel proud and happy. I literally always salute Isshin, in his last boss fight. He asks you to be better and even tips you. Hesitation is defeat, if other fromsoft games made you play the game passively, this game is allegory for life. Move towards hardships, don't stop. If you die, keep trying don't give up. It'll click soon, and when it clicks it will be the best feeling you will experience in your life.
Easily the best Souls-borne game mechanics and the lore is no slouch

Played this many years ago and finally got a chance to replay it in 2026. This is hands-down one of the best games out their. Even being a Dark Souls fan, Sekiro really stands unique way that makes it very re-playable (even without the rpg mechanics of Dark Souls). This is a game that requires patience (and the game even accommodates for that), aggression during boss encounters, and learning from failure. If you want a good challenging that will run around 20-30 hours at the most, this is a great get at a discount. If you are a new FromSoftware fan, just buy the game regardless of discount - you will continue to enjoy what makes their games very unique.

Performance is great with no crashes or bugs on my end. If you end up uncapping the frame rates with a mod like I did, do expect some weird bugs during combat.

Score: 5/5 (Buy full price)
